Four closeout moves.

1) Dembe (short email, sent to her directly):
"Dembe — I didn't want you to leave without saying this properly. The access-matrix you built in month two is what turned Workstream B from a mess into something we could actually design against — I went back to it constantly. Beyond that, you were straight with me even when the news was awkward, which made the whole ten months easier. Wishing you the best at the new place. I'd genuinely like to stay in touch — no agenda, just glad to have worked with you."

2) Sam's credit (note to the vendor PM, cc R. Adekanmi):
"Quick correction on something from the closeout: the dashboard rebuild you praised was substantially Sam Okafor's work — Sam led it end to end. The praise should land with Sam by name, not 'the PC team.' Sam didn't ask me to send this; that's rather the point." I'll also tell Sam directly that I flagged it, and tell R. that Sam's ready for more.

3) Maya (LinkedIn/email, two lines):
"Maya — saw you made programme director, congratulations, thoroughly deserved. I still use the way you ran sponsor pre-reads — it stuck with me. Really pleased for you." No ask.

4) Victor:
The pattern here is consistent, not a one-off — repeated credit-taking and throwing a junior under the bus in steering. That's the kind of association I don't want and won't vouch for. I won't confront him or make it dramatic; I'll just reply courteously that I'm not able to take on the bid and leave it there. I won't put a word in with the director — quietly declining is the honest move. From here I'll stay professional if we cross paths but I won't invest in the relationship, and it can fade.

Rationale: take the moments that are right (credit Sam, reconnect with Dembe and Maya) for their own sake; let the toxic relationship thin without burning it.
